coredata.dc:title: Different functional groups of carbon dots influence the formation of protein crowns and pepsin characteristic in vitro digestion
coredata.dc:creator: Lei, Chunli
Tao, Mengna
Xu, Lanqing
Yue, Le
Cao, Xuesong
Cheng, Bingxu
Wang, Chuanxi
Wang, Zhenyu
coredata.dc:description: Application of nanomaterials (NMs) in agriculture poses an ingestion risk to humans and may affect the digestive process. Different fates of NMs with differential charges in the gastrointestinal tract should be considered. In this study, the interaction between three carbon dots (CDs) carried with different functional groups (–NH2, –OH, and –COOH) and pepsin was analyzed through an in vitro digestion model. The results showed that CDs significantly reduced pepsin activity. Among them, CDs–NH2 had the greatest effect, following by CDs–OH, and CDs–COOH. Besides, molecular docking demonstrated the specific binding site of CDs to pepsin, while the most stable binding energy (–8.10 kcal/mol) was formed between CDs–NH2 and pepsin. Further, CDs formed a nanomaterial–protein crown structure with pepsin. The present study enriches the functional group properties of CDs in the digestion and provides new ideas for the potential human health of NMs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Functional groups
Pepsin
In vitro digestion
Human health

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Assay of pepsin and trypsin activity
Molecular docking analysis of the interaction between functional groups and pepsin
Effect of CDs–COOH, CDs–NH2, CDs–OH on pepsin activity
Analysis of changes in gastric digestion process of CDs with different functional groups
Functional groups mediate the effect of CDs on the structure of pepsin
Molecular mechanism analysis of the action of CDs on pepsin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effects of different concentrations (5, 10, and 20 mg/L) of CDs–NH2, CDs–OH, CDs–COOH on pepsin activity after incubation with pepsin. Gray boxes: blank control group without added CDs; blue boxes: treated group with 5 mg/L CDs; green boxes: treated group with 10 mg/L CDs; pink boxes: treated group with 20 mg/L CDs. Red stars indicate the mean of the data of each group. Different letters indicate statistically significant (p < 0.05) intra–group experimental differences between groups. Black asterisks indicate statistically significant differences in in vitro experiments relative to the control group (p < 0.05). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The number of granules in digestive fluid changed before and after digestion. (a) Fluorescent response changes of FSC–A/SSC–A signaling channel after incubation of CDs in in vitro gastric juice system; (b) Particle number changes of SSC–A/Count signaling channel after incubation of CDs in in vitro gastric juice system; (c) SSC–A/Time signaling channel changes after incubation of CDs in in vitro gastric juice system.

Effect of CDs–NH2, CDs–OH, CDs–COOH on the structure of pepsin. (a). UV absorption spectra of pepsin. (b). Three–dimensional fluorescence spectrum. (c). FTIR structure of pepsin. (d). Circular dichroism spectrum of pepsin. The gray lines represent the control group without CDs; The blue lines indicate exposure of CDs-NH2 to an in vitro gastric fluid simulation system; The green lines indicate exposure of CDs-OH to an in vitro gastric fluid simulation system; The red lines indicate exposure of CDs-COOH to an in vitro gastric fluid simulation system. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Molecular docking model of pepsin with (a) CDs–NH2, (b) CDs–OH, (c) CDs–COOH. Gray sticks indicate H atoms; blue sticks indicate N atoms; red sticks indicate O atoms; and light blue indicates amino acid sites in the pepsin structure that bind specifically to the functional groups carried by different CDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

AFM image of (a) pepsin, (b) CDs–NH2, (c) CDs–OH, (d) CDs–COOH incubated with pepsin. The left panel indicates the morphological images of CDs after simulated incubation with in vitro gastric juice; the right panel shows the corresponding 3D images.

head.ce:title: Different functional groups of carbon dots influence the formation of protein crowns and pepsin characteristic in vitro digestion

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Processes and Pollution Control, and School of Environment and Ecology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Application of nanomaterials (NMs) in agriculture poses an ingestion risk to humans and may affect the digestive process. Different fates of NMs with differential charges in the gastrointestinal tract should be considered. In this study, the interaction between three carbon dots (CDs) carried with different functional groups (–NH2, –OH, and –COOH) and pepsin was analyzed through an in vitro digestion model. The results showed that CDs significantly reduced pepsin activity. Among them, CDs–NH2 had the greatest effect, following by CDs–OH, and CDs–COOH. Besides, molecular docking demonstrated the specific binding site of CDs to pepsin, while the most stable binding energy (–8.10 kcal/mol) was formed between CDs–NH2 and pepsin. Further, CDs formed a nanomaterial–protein crown structure with pepsin. The present study enriches the functional group properties of CDs in the digestion and provides new ideas for the potential human health of NMs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid development of nano–enable–agriculture has not only contributed to the increase of crop production, but also dramatically improved their quality, providing great advantages for the application of sustainable agricultural technologies (Wang et al., 2022a). Application of Fe7(PO4)6 and Fe3O4 nanomaterials (NMs) (10 mg/L) effectively enhanced the nutrient content of soybean (>14.0 %) (Wang et al., 2022c). Foliar spraying nitrogen (N)–doped carbon dots (CDs) at the equivalent concentration on maize eventually yielded an improvement in seed quality (50.2 %) (Wang et al., 2021). Moreover, many reports indicated the accumulation of multiple NMs (e.g. CeO2, La2O3, and Fe3O4) in various plants, inevitably increasing the risk of oral ingestion in humans (Cao et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022b). In recent years, researchers seem to be intentionally focusing on the effects of NMs, as widely demonstrated in several vivo and vitro experiments (Chen et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2022a). Setyawati et al. (2013) certificated that TiO2 NMs (23.5 nm) induced endothelial cell leakage in only 30 min under concentrations as low as 10 μM. Song et al., 2022) covered that carbon NMs can be absorbed by the digestive system and transported with body fluids, and affect the digestive system and even pose potential health risks to the liver, spleen, kidney and lungs. Therefore, after NMs is ingested by the organism, and their series of changes in the body become critical to assess their effects on human health and safety.

CDs (with UV absorption, antioxidant and antimicrobial properties, etc.) is a particularly attractive class of agriculturally available NMs, and has been well known for their beneficial effects on crop growth (Liu et al., 2020). Nevertheless, few studies have reported the human health risk following oral administration, and their safety assessment is particularly necessary (Di Cristo et al., 2021). The available evidence has been providing that low concentrations (2 mg/kg) of CDs might not appear to significantly reduce cellular activity or mammalian health in the short time frame (Li et al., 2021). However, Cao et al. (2021) reported that the CDs (39 μmol/L) significantly reduced the enzymatic activity of hemoglobin. Notably, CDs are typically susceptible to chemical functionalization by loading a high density of nitrogen– or oxygen–containing functional groups (Miao et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2022b). These properties of NMs at the microscale create highly reactive surfaces that readily bind functional molecules through chemical bonding (Mahmoudi et al., 2023). Arezki et al. (2022) demonstrated that the physicochemical properties and biological functions of the protein corona formed by CDs and proteins depend on the charge properties of NMs. Nonetheless, the existence of specific binding between functional CDs and various biomolecules in vivo has not been investigated, and the microscopic mechanism is still in a gap.

The stomach, a key part of the digestive system, is responsible for digesting any food ingested through the mouth and has a complex physiological process. In particular, the interaction with pepsin, an important enzyme involved in the digestive process in the stomach (Linkov et al., 2011). However, knowledge is still limited about the close relationship between CDs and pepsin. Hence, an in–depth investigation of their mechanism of action is essential to understand the impact of CDs on human health. In present study, physiological conditions were simulated and investigated the interaction between CDs loaded with different functional groups on the initial digestion stage–pepsin. The effect of the variability of functional groups on CDs on pepsin was analyzed by examining the number of particles before and after digestion and morphological changes. In addition, the binding sites and covalent binding ability of CDs with different functional groups to amino acids in pepsin were revealed by molecular docking. This study provides fundamental information for evaluating the potential health effects of NMs based agricultural application on humans.

Pepsin (pig gastric mucosa, 1:3000 USP) and trypsin (pig pancreas, 250 USP U/mg) were purchased from Sigma (Steinbach, Germany). Other chemical reagents are purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., LTD. (Shanghai, China).

CDs–NH2, CDs–OH and CDs–COOH were prepared by hydrothermal synthesis using urea, glucose and citric acid as raw materials. The morphological images of the CDs were obtained by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM–2100, Nippon Electronics Co, JPN). The elemental analyses of the CDs were performed by X–ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, EscaLab 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, US). The surface functional groups of CDs were characterized using fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Tensor 27, Bruker Optics, Germany). Details of the preparation and purification process of CDs–NH2, CDs–OH and CDs–COOH and sample measurements were described in the Supporting information (Text S1). The characterization results of various CDs were shown in Fig. S1.

In vitro gastrointestinal digestion experiments were performed as described by Versantvoort et al. (2005). This procedure was applied under fasting conditions (i.e., without any food) and consisted of a two-step, simulated digestive process in two phases, i.e., gastric, and intestinal fluids. All two phases had different salt and enzyme compositions, which was listed in Table S1. The digestion process of three CDs with different functional groups (CDs–COOH, CDs–NH2, CDs–OH) at different concentrations (5, 10, and 20 mg/L) was analyzed. Solutions without adding CDs were used as control (CK). Briefly, the artificial gastric fluid and intestinal juice were respectively preconfigured in a warm bath at 37 °C, and prepared aqueous solutions of different concentrations of CDs were mixed 1:1 with the artificial fluid, with the final concentrations of CDs to 5, 10, and 20 mg/L, respectively. Then they were shaken (100 r/min) for 2 h at 37 °C. A portion of the product was collected for subsequent analysis, and the other portion was used directly in the subsequent digestion of the intestinal fluid for 4 h at 37 °C. The digestive fluid collected at each stage of digestion was filtered through a 0.45 µm membrane filter, and the filtrate was collected and analyzed by flow cytometry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of pepsin and trypsin activity
ce:section.ce:para: The enzymatic activity of pepsin was assayed according to the method reported by Zhang et al. (2020). Ater the first stage of gastric digestion, 5 mL of the digested solution and 500 μL of bovine hemoglobin solution was mixed, and the solution was warmed in a 37 °C water column for 10 min. 500 μL of 5 % trichloroacetic acid was added to each test solution to stop the reaction. Then followed by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was removed and the optical density of the supernatant was measured at 275 nm, and the relative activity of pepsin was calculated. The trypsin activity described in the Supporting information (Text S2).

Three–dimensional (3D) fluorescence analysis using F–2700 spectrometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). The reaction solution was collected and dried into solid samples for circular dichroism spectroscopy as well as FTIR spectroscopy to analyze the secondary structure changes of pepsin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular docking analysis of the interaction between functional groups and pepsin
ce:section.ce:para: The structural formulae of three CDs loaded with different functional groups (CDs–COOH, CDs–NH2, CDs–OH) were described and constructed separately for intermolecular covalent interactions with pepsin. Firstly, the structures of three molecules used for the synthesis of CDs–citric acid, urea and glucose were obtained from the chemical structure website (https://pubchem.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/), and the structural prediction of CDs was performed (Fig. S2). The three–dimensional molecular structures of pepsin were obtained from the Molecular Library (https://www.rcsb.org/). Docking was performed separately in Autodock software. The final docking results were visualized in the Pymol software.

Morphological studies of CDs and pepsin (CDs–pepsin) complexes were studied by AFM. Pepsin solution and CDs–pepsin solutions were filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane, dropped on mica sheets, and dried at room temperature. Samples were imaged in dynamic force mode (DFM).

All data were presented as the mean ± SD with three determinations. Origin 2023 software was used for experimental data processing and graph production. The SPSS software version 22.0 was used for Duncan's test to determine the identity of the group in which significant differences were observed. Values of p < 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs–COOH, CDs–NH2, CDs–OH on pepsin activity
ce:section.ce:para: Detailed characterization of CDs–COOH, CDs–NH2, and CDs–OH confirmed the presence of surface groups (Text S3 and Fig. S1). Considering that CDs–COOH, CDs–NH2, and CDs–OH were affected by changes in pH throughout the digestion process, the zeta potential changes of CDs–COOH, CDs–NH2, and CDs–OH were determined and analyzed under different pH conditions (Fig. S3). The results showed that at gastric fluid related pH (pH = 1.5), CDs–COOH, CDs–NH2, and CDs–OH exhibited colloidal stability. This may be due to the fact that low pH makes the nanoparticles unstable and prone to aggregation (Zhang et al., 2022). In addition, our results showed that the zeta change of CDs-NH2 seemed to present an increase of the positive zeta potential with pH. CDs-OH, on the contrary, showed a gradual decrease in zeta with increasing pH, while a moderate rise was observed at pH = 8.5. The results of this study were likely to be the consequence of a combination of factors, both of the material itself as well as the ionic strength and pH of the water. (Kamaly et al., 2012). And then, the pepsin activity decreased after the CDs were mixed into the gastric juice, regardless of the effect of functional group and concentration (Fig. 1 and Table S2). In detail, the pepsin activity decreased with increasing concentrations of CDs, and this did not produce significant differences between CDs–COOH, CDs–NH2, and CDs–OH (Fig. 1). Interestingly, it seems that the effect of functional group specificity on its activity was more intuitive, especially at high concentrations. Compared to CK, the pepsin activity was reduced by 9.4 % after CDs–NH2 treatment at 20 mg/L and by 7.6 % after treatment with CDs–OH, while the CDs–COOH group decreased by only 3.5 % (Fig. 1). The results indicated that all three kinds of CDs could reduce the activity of pepsin, but at the same time, it was closely related to the difference of functional groups. The sulfidation–induced increase in the negative charge of Ag and ZnO NMs enhances the electrostatic repulsion between the membrane–linked proteins and NMs, ultimately reducing the interaction between them (Ault et al., 2016; Carnovale et al., 2021). Furthermore, a recent study reported that the binding capacity of CDs to serum proteins showed a strong correlation with the loading of different amounts of –NH2 groups (Arezki et al., 2022). Therefore, functional groups are key factors in the role of CDs in the regulation of various physiologies in vivo. However, it seems that the investigation of the differential binding of CDs with different functional groups to biological proteins in vivo has yet to provide insights. In contrast, gastrointestinal digestion deserves attention as a necessary pathway for food–derived CDs to enter the body from the environment. Among them, pepsin as a key factor of digestion should be analyzed with emphasis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of changes in gastric digestion process of CDs with different functional groups
ce:section.ce:para: The changes in particle numbers of three CDs, CDs–NH2, CDs–OH, and CDs–COOH, before and after digestion with gastric juice were monitored (Text S4, Figs. 2 , and S4). The density distribution showed a significant decrease in the density of particles in their reaction system occurred after incubation with CDs, especially CDs–NH2 (Fig. 2a). The quantitative statistics showed that the number of CDs–NH2 treated group was only half of the control treated group after digestion half of CK, further confirming the significance of the changes (Fig. 2b). For pepsin solution incubated with CDs–OH, the total number of particles in the solution decreased by 37.3 % after gastric digestion, while that in CDs–COOH incubated group only decreased by 11.7 %. The change in quantity after digestion was partly due to the agglomeration behavior of the CDs themselves under extremely acidic conditions, and on the other hand, the interactions between the CDs and pepsin cause them to form larger agglomerates resulting in a decrease in the number of particles (Zuo et al., 2013). More intensive protein encapsulation was observed in larger NMs compared to smaller particles. Lacerda et al. (2010) identified that the strength of interaction of citrate–encapsulated Au NMs with human blood proteins increased with the size of the NMs and, more deeply, that surface charge screening was more effective for larger particles compared to smaller ones. Moreover, the analysis of particle size further explains the reason for the decrease of particle number. The DLS results showed that the particle size of pepsin solution had a tendency to increase gradually after incubation with CDs loaded with three different functional groups (Fig. S5a). Among them, the grain size of natural pepsin solution was 680.4 nm after digestion, and that of CDs–NH2, CDs–OH and CDs–COOH treatment groups increased to 1403.7 nm, 1134.0 nm and 963.9 nm after digestion of gastric juices, respectively. The increase in particle size resulted in their retention in the filter membrane without being detected by flow cytometry, and ultimately a decrease in the number of particles in the reaction system, in agreement with the above results. Previous studies have also indicated that NMs will covalently bind to pepsin when passing through gastric fluid and affect the normal physiological function of pepsin (Dudefoi et al., 2021; Martín-Hernández et al., 2021;). Besides, the change of zeta potential after the addition of CDs in pepsin solution can also prove the interaction between CDs and pepsin (Fig. 5b). For the original artificial gastric fluid, the particles in the system were only slightly cationic, i.e., the zeta potential value was + 5.3 mV. After incubation with CDs, the zeta potential value of the artificial gastric fluid system showed a decrease, demonstrating that the adsorbed or free CDs contributed to the electrical signal. The positive charge magnitude of the system decreased after exposure, attributed to variations in surface charge or to electrostatic screening effects. In particular, CDs–NH2 showed a more pronounced decrease, attributing to the fact that aniline protonation changes the molecular state of CDs and the electrostatic charge on pepsin, while high ionic strength leads to an electrostatic screening effect of the surface charge (Tangsrianugul et al., 2015). Previous experimental data clearly showed that positively charged NMs consistently attracted more proteins when placed in biological medium, regardless of the type of material used (inorganic, polymer core–shell NMs, and liposomes) (Burnand et al., 2018; Cheng et al., 2019). Marchetti et al. (2015) found that functionalization of the moiety enhanced the binding of carbon nanotubes and polystyrene NMs to active proteins on the surface of alveolar epithelial cells, leading to the incorporation of larger sized NMs–protein complexes. Hence, the potential interaction between CDs–NH2 and pepsin was stronger than that between CDs–OH and CDs–COOH, resulting in significant changes in the number of particles in the total digestion process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functional groups mediate the effect of CDs on the structure of pepsin
ce:section.ce:para: The changes in the UV absorption spectra showed a decrease in the absorbance of pepsin at 275 nm due to the addition of CDs (Fig. 3 a), confirming that pepsin modifies the pepsin microenvironment around the aromatic residues in the presence of CDs. It was further found that CDs–NH2–treated pepsin showed the most significant decrease in its UV absorption spectrum compared to CK, followed by CDs–OH, CDs–COOH (Fig. 3a). Pepsin has an intrinsic absorption peak near 206 nm in response to the CO structure of its own peptide backbone and shows a dense band, while the presence of a weak absorption peak at 275 nm is caused by the chromophores of phenylalanine, tryptophan and tyrosine residues in the pepsin structure (Tan et al., 2019). Apparently, the addition of CDs altered the release of amino acids from the pepsin chromophore. Thus, pepsin undergoes structural changes in the presence of CDs, and CDs–NH2 had a prominent effect.

Furthermore, CDs may covalently bind to pepsin and thus change its spatial conformation, which was confirmed by three–dimensional fluorescence contour map. In the presence of CDs, the fluorescence intensity of pepsin decreased significantly, which indicated that CDs resulted in quenching of pepsin fluorescence and the changed the conformational change of pepsin, especially in the pepsin treatment group with CDs–NH2 (Fig. 3b). In general, natural pepsin presents two typical fluorescence peaks due to the n–π* transition (λex = 240 nm, λem = 345 nm) of the carbon double bond in the protein characteristic polypeptide skeleton structure and the intrinsic fluorescence properties of tryptophan and tyrosine residues (λex = 280 nm, λem = 345 nm) (He et al., 2007). The entire phenomenon and analytical data demonstrated that the binding of CDs to pepsin its interaction resulted in a substantial unfolding of the pepsin polypeptide backbone structure. After adding CDs, more hydrophobic tryptophan residues were buried in the hydrophobic pocket of pepsin, and the conformation of pepsin was markedly changed (Guo et al., 2023). Obviously, the microenvironment (CDs–NH2, CDs–OH and CDs–COOH incubation) changed the maximum emission wavelength and fluorescence intensity of pepsin residues (Trp or Tyr), resulting in potential structural changes of pepsin.

FTIR provides a means to analyze the spectroscopic changes in the structural backbone and polar side chains of pepsin (Fig. 3c). The amide Ⅰ band was the main vibrational band of pepsin, and its CO stretching vibrational peak appeared at 1633 cm−1, and its position peak did not show significant shift in the presence of CDs–COOH, while the position of the sharp peak shifted to 1624 cm−1, and 1622 cm−1 in the presence of CDs–OH and CDs–NH2, respectively, indicating that the CO group in the pepsin polypeptide backbone was involved in the interaction with CDs. Huang et al. (2016) demonstrated that the interaction of CDs with human serum albumin induced rearrangement of the human serum albumin secondary structure, and that CDs affected not only the stretching vibration of the CO group in the protein polypeptide, but also the CN stretching vibration and the NH bending vibration (Hill et al., 2016). Notably, the FTIR of pepsin remained highly similar after the addition of CDs, indicating that the basic features of the secondary structure of pepsin were retained even in the presence of CDs. The interaction between CDs and pepsin may alternate between the protein skeleton as well as the polar side chains (Song et al., 2019). The normal function of pepsin is based on hydrophobic interactions, hydrogen bonds and ionic interactions to maintain relative stability, the addition of CDs has upset this stable state. In general, the results of activity inhibition and characteristic spectrum confirmed that CDs inevitably alter the structure of pepsin by conformational changes.

The circular dichroism spectroscopy results further showed the effect of CDs incorporation on the secondary structure of pepsin (Fig. 3d). In maintaining consistency with the described results, the addition of CDs–NH2 had the most significant effect on pepsin. Specifically, the helical structure of pepsin was reduced and the proportion of β–sheet components was decreased by 10.9 % (Fig. S6). In comparison, the pepsin treatment groups with the addition of CDs–OH and CDs–COOH decreased by 6.3 % and 4.1 %, respectively. In addition, the random fractions in the CDs–NH2, CDs–OH and CDs–COOH groups increased by 13.8 %, 7.4 % and 5.1 %, respectively, compared with CK, indicating that the proportion of disordered regions in the spatial structure of pepsin increased with the addition of CDs (Fig. S6). The activity of pepsin is determined by its specific spatial conformation, which depends heavily on the covalent and non–covalent interactions between amino acid residues in the pepsin structure. A previous result obtained similar results for the involvement of gold NMs in the modulation of the secondary conformation of trypsin (Lacerda et al., 2010). Moreover, Toro–Mendoza et al. (2023) confirmed that the binding ability of polyethylene glycol (PEG) polymeric coated Au NMs loaded with different functional groups (–NH2, –COOH, and –OCH3) to human serum albumin (HSA) and apoferritin (Apo) was demonstrated to show the difference in functional groups, with higher targeted binding energy between –NH2 and protein than –COOH. Clearly, the possible reason for our results lies in the fact that the specific interactions formed between CDs and pepsin from the incubation of CDs cause relatively weak non–covalent interactions between the peptide chains in pepsin to change. However, the presence of amino groups showed more extensive binding capacity in covalent interaction, resulting in prominent changes in the structure of pepsin after incubation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular mechanism analysis of the action of CDs on pepsin
ce:section.ce:para: The results of molecular docking showed the binding energies of CDs–NH2, CDs–OH, and CDs–COOH to pepsin were –8.1 kcal/mol, –4.4 kcal/mol, and –1.6 kcal/mol, respectively (Fig. 4 ). Normally, when the molecular binding energy is at 0 kcal/mol, the mutual binding process between CDs and pepsin proceeds spontaneously (Zhang et al., 2022b). And when the binding energy is < –1.2 kcal/mol, the binding state proved to be stable (Shemetov et al., 2012). The trend of the binding ability of the three CDs to pepsin was consistent in terms of the binding energy. The smaller the binding energy the more affinity between the CDs and the target amino acid residues of pepsin. Hence, CDs–NH2 could form a more solid binding to pepsin, followed by CDs–OH, and the least binding was CDs–COOH. Additionally, based on the docking results, it was clear that CDs–NH2 appeared to be embedded in the binding cavity formed by the α–helix of pepsin and hydrogen bonds which were formed between the –NH2 loaded on CDs and alanine (Ala); the –OH in the structure of CDs–OH combined with asparagine (Asn) and lysine (Lys) in pepsin to form double hydrogen bond; –COOH in CDs–COOH bound to Lys to form a single hydrogen bond and tends to the marginalization site of the protein (Fig. 4). The specificity of amino acid sites in pepsin bound by different functional groups was the underlying factor for the variability in non–covalent binding ability. Therefore, the specific non–covalent binding between CDs loaded with different functional groups and pepsin leads to continuous aggregation between them, and the formation of biological protein crowns.

Subsequently, the aggregation and morphological changes of CDs after interaction with pepsin were confirmed by AFM images (Fig. 5 ). The natural pepsin appeared as a small spherical particle, and the three–dimensional AFM image showed a clear form of pepsin with a height of about 3 nm (Fig. 5). Crystallographic analysis showed that the three–dimensional lengths of pepsin are about 6.0, 4.0, and 2.7 nm, respectively. Compared with the native pepsin, the size of the complexed CDs and pepsin increased, especially the CDs–NH2 complexed with pepsin, which was about 30 nm, while the particle size of the complexed pepsin incubated with CDs–OH was about 20 nm, respectively. Interestingly, the addition of CDs–COOH appeared to exhibit insignificant intercalation on the complex formation, and the AFM results showed that although the exact composition of the particle size could not be accurately determined, most of the particles < 5 nm were still present in the incubated solution, suggesting a weak complexation of CDs–COOH with pepsin. the size and significant changes in the morphological characteristics of the complexes formed by CDs and pepsin were associated with the adhesion of CDs on the protein surface, which in turn aggregated protein particles or produced CDs protein crowns. A recent study clarified that pepsin can act as a bridge between carbon–based NMs, causing strong agglomeration and formation of protein crowns (Tang et al., 2023). Separately, functional group properties play a dominant role in determining the affinity of nanoparticles to biomolecules, and ultimately in determining the formation of protein crowns. Rampado et al. (2020) found that the loading of functional groups with hydrophilic motifs promoted the formation of hydration layers, thus reducing the interaction between the surface of NMs and proteins and other biomolecules. Therefore, this phenomenon was consistent with the results of analysis simulation, CDs broken the original stable equilibrium system of pepsin, forming molecular bonding, resulting in the formation of protein caps between CDs and pepsin. CDs–NH2 showed the most stable binding state.

The digestive process in the gastric juice is accompanied by the entry into the intestinal digestive system immediately after the digestion process. Hence, it is also necessary to understand the digestive behavior of CDs in the intestinal fluid. The results of the simulations of intestinal fluid showed that the number of particles dissolved in the digested digestive fluid species also showed a tendency to decrease after digestion by intestinal fluid, suggesting that CDs were likely to be involved in relevant physiological processes during the digestion of intestinal fluid (Fig. S7a). The diminished activity of trypsin was confirmed after the assay of trypsin activity, which further verifies the effect of CDs on important active substances in the intestinal tract (Fig. S7). More specifically, CDs–COOH seem to have a prominent influence on the effects during the digestion of intestinal fluids, which is contrary to the results found during the digestion of gastric fluids. Thus, the effects exhibited by CDs at different stages of digestion are closely related to the nature of the functional groups they carry, and their processes are complex and variable. Therefore, when exploring the effects of NMs on human gastrointestinal digestion and even human health, their own properties need to be focused on.

In summary, this study evaluated the effects of CDs on the digestive system in terms of their possible functional group conformation. In vitro experiments showed that the addition of CDs reduced the activity and changed the properties of pepsin, and the variability of the functional groups was more significant. Fluorescence experiments showed that the intrinsic fluorescence of pepsin was statically burst by CDs. the interaction between CDs and pepsin also reduced the number of β–sheets in the protein structure of pepsin. the morphology and size of the complex formed between CDs and pepsin confirmed its corona structure, and the effect of CDs–NH2 on pepsin was most prominent due to the structural modification of the functional groups. From the results of in vitro molecular simulations, the ability of CDs with different functional groups to covalently bind to amino acid residues of pepsin is the underlying reason for the effect of CDs on pepsin. Thus, these results draw attention to the effects of NMs on the digestive system during agricultural production and provide insights into the interaction between NMs and the digestive system, as well as a potential assessment of human health.

Chunli Lei: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Mengna Tao: Data curation, Investigation. Lanqing Xu: Data curation, Investigation. Le Yue: Writing – review & editing. Xuesong Cao: Writing – review & editing. Bingxu Cheng: Data curation, Investigation. Chuanxi Wang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Zhenyu Wang: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (42192572, 42322705, and 41820104009), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (JUSRP622019 and JUSRP221023), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province, China (BK20230044).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.138224.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
